is an old ice cream shop that has been operating on this street for over 50 years. They offer a variety of Boran ice cream or Thai traditional ice cream on sticks with a wide range of flavors to choose from.

is truly one of the most iconic seaside mountains in Thailand. The Thai name, "Sam Roi Yot", can be translated as "300 peaks", which is something you will see right away as you approach the national park.

For one, it is also home to , one of the most iconic seaside mountains in Thailand and one of my favorite places in Thailand. At the national park, you can Sam Roi Yot Lotus Pond, a vast and beautiful lotus pond located photogenically on the foothill of Sam Roi Yot Mountain, Khao Daeng Viewpoint, a stunning viewpoint you can hike to, and so much more.

There are several tourist attractions around the national park you can visit. For the best viewpoint of the park, you can drive to  where you will be able to get an unobstructed view of the huge lake around Sam Roi Yot National Park, and the mountain itself looming behind in its full glory.

Another place you should check out is , a vast and beautiful lotus pond located photogenically at the foothill of Sam Roi Yot Mountain. Here, you will find a nice walking path that goes above the lotus pond with several salas (rest stops) for you to enjoy.

Looking for a bit more adventure at Sam Roi Yot National Park? Not too far from Sam Phraya Beach, you'll find the , a breathtaking trail that you can hike up to the summit of Khao Daeng Mountain. From up there, you will get a stunning panoramic view of the coastline of Sam Roi Yot in its entirety.
One of the most magical sights you can experience in Prachuap Khiri Khan, if not the whole of Thailand, is when the light shines through a cavern illuminating a royal throne inside .

You can also visit , a large cavern located deep in the mountain where you will find a royal throne built right in the middle of the cave, with lights shining through the hole from the top, illuminating the throne. It is quite a sight to behold.
To get to Phraya Nakhon Cave, you will have to drive to  where you can then, take a 5-minute long-tail boat journey to the trailhead before you can begin the hike to Phraya Nakhon Cave.

You have probably seen a Thai Elephant before, probably in one of the many elephant sanctuaries here but have you ever seen them in the wild? You can do just that at  where wild elephants still roam free between the foothills of the mighty Tenasserim mountain range.

If you are traveling solo or as a couple, you can wait for others to join which shouldn't be much of a problem on the weekends but you might have to wait a while if you are there on the weekdays. There are 2 entrances to the park but only  is where you can go on a safari trip so make sure you get to the right one. I went to the wrong one and had to backtrack more than 30 minutes to get here, so don't be like me 😅.
As you make your way down south, you will find the , a beautiful temple located in the remote area of the province where you can hike to a cave nearby with several Buddha images inside.

Located right in the heart of Prachuap Khiri Khan's city, you will find , a hilltop temple with a great view of the entire coastal area. Oh, and you will find a ton of monkeys along the way that you have to wade through. Like, A TON of them!
In Prachuap Khiri Khan City, you can also hike through a monkey-ridden trail to get to the top of , a hilltop temple with a great view of the entire coastal area.

One of my favorite beaches in Thailand is  (or Ao Manao in Thai) located inside the premise of the Wing 5 Air Base, where the air force resided which may sound pretty intimidating but that is actually a good thing, being under military care.

Near the city, you will also find one of my favorite beaches in Thailand, , a spacious and clean beach with crystal-clear and shallow water, perfect for swimming, set against the beautiful rolling limestone hills of Prachuap Khiri Khan's diverse landscape.

While you visit Lemon Bay, be sure to also spend some time and explore all the tourist attractions inside  like the Monkey Monument where you will be able to interact with the lutungs or leaf monkeys, a prettier, friendlier primate than the ones at Wat Khao Chong Krachok.
At the , you will often find a ton of these beautiful monkeys hanging out in the trees waiting for you to feed them with nuts and rice.

Also located inside the Wing 5 Air Base, you will find , a small and fun local aquarium where you can experience the rich marine ecosystem of Thailand up close and see some of the rarest fish you won't get to see so easily like Lionfish and more.
Probably one of the most iconic and photogenic temples in Prachuap Khiri Khan, , is a colorful Buddhist temple located on top of a hill overlooking the beautiful pristine coastline of the province.
While you are at the temple, you can also go for a stroll along , a beautiful quiet beach, or try some delicious seafood dishes at Platoo Seafood, a nice seaside restaurant nearby.
And of course, since you are in Prachuap Khiri Khan, you might as well drop by  and see what the fuzz is about and why the city is so popular among Bangkokians and tourists alike.

There are also several other interesting tourist attractions that are not too far from Hua Hin you can visit, such as  and its incredible viewpoint, Pran Buri Forest Park, a great place to stroll around the mangrove forest, and the beautiful beach of Khao Kalok, which is definitely one of my favorite beaches here.

If you have time left, you can take your scooter even further into the Erawan national park and visit  where you will find plenty of beautiful rock formations, with one looking like a Buddha image and another that looks like a coral reef.
You can continue further and end your first day at the  where you will be able to relax at the Srinagarind Dam recreational area and enjoy the expansive view of the Srinagarind Dam from the ridge.

After visiting Mallika, if you want to see a train crossing the  wooden trestle, which is a popular section of the Death Railway, you will have to get to  Train Station before 1:30 PM. You should be able to reach there in 30 minutes from Mallika via a scooter, so plan your time carefully.

place: Asia Saiyok Express Bus Company office
description: To get from Kanchanaburi to Sangkhlaburi, you will have to get to the , located right behind the Kanchanaburi Bus Terminal. From there, you can buy a ticket to Sangkhlaburi.

place: The Asia Saiyok Express Bus Company
description: operates several microbuses a day, connecting Kanchanaburi City to the Three Pagoda Pass at the Thailand-Myanmar border, where they pass through Sangkhlaburi.

The microbus runs from 5 AM - 7 PM, approximately every 45 minutes or so. Once the bus arrives in Sangklaburi, they will drop you off at , which is also the same place where you can catch your bus back.
To get from Sangklaburi to Pilok, all you have to do is go to  and get a minibus to Thong Pha Phum.

is a small town tucked away between the forests and mountains of the Tenasserim Hills, a mountain range that dominates much of southern and central Thailand, creating a natural border between Thailand and Myanmar.

To observe the alms-giving ceremony in Sangklaburi, you will have to wake up at 6 AM and cross the  to the main road of the Mon Village by 6:30 AM to see monks making their way down to the bridge. The monks will not cross the bridge like they used to, as it has become way too crowded, so do not expect them to be on the Thai side of town.

After you are done with the alms-giving ceremony, you can then spend some time walking along the  and interacting with the locals. The  is often crowded in the morning, especially after the alms-giving ceremony, and you will find many Mon/Thai kids selling flowers and Burmese-style powders for a few Baht.

After the boat tour, you can then spend the rest of your day walking around the Mon village up the hill and checking out the newly rebuilt temples like  and Chedi Phutthakhaya which are definitely worthy of their old sunken temple names.
On your second day in Sangklaburi, go rent a scooter and start your day by visiting the historic  that connects India to the rest of Southeast Asia.

After visiting the Three Pagoda Pass, you can ride down to Sangkhlaburi and visit the  in Khao Laem National Park.  is a stunning camping ground and rest area where you will be able to get an unobstructed view of the Vajiralongkorn Dam. It is around 35km away from Sangkhlaburi and it should take you about 45 minutes to get there via a scooter.

Last but not least, you can continue further down the road and visit , a beautiful collection of small waterfalls where you can walk around or sit by a café nearby and enjoy the view before heading back to Sangkhlaburi to end your day.

place: a bus stop along the road near Thong Pha Phum town
description: Once you arrive in Thong Pha Phum, they will drop you off at . This is not the bus stop to get to Pilok. The actual bus stop to get to Pilok is located at Thong Pha Phum Food Market and you will have to get a moto-taxi to drop you off there for 20 THB.

Once you arrive in Thong Pha Phum, they will drop you off at a bus stop along the road near Thong Pha Phum town. This is not the bus stop to get to Pilok. The actual bus stop to get to Pilok is located at  and you will have to get a moto-taxi to drop you off there for 20 THB.

Once you arrive at , you will see several yellow Song-Taew parked in the parking lot at the entrance of the market. Simply go there and ask the guy which Song-Taew goes to Pilok, and they will direct you to the right one.

is a small, old mining village tucked away in the rolling hills and mountains of the Tenasserim Hills, which creates a natural border between Thailand and Myanmar. The reason why I added Pilok to the itinerary is to allow you to see the wilder, less tamed side of Kanchanaburi province.

There are several things you can easily do within the Baan I'Tong village that do not require prior planning, such as taking a walk up to the top of , where you will get a nice view of the landscape surrounding the village.

You can also check out the  where you can still find some old pieces of machinery left lying around in the area. There's also a nice koi pond there with one of the most colorful carp fish I have ever seen.

The highlight of visiting Pilok is to see , a 30m-high mighty waterfall where the water flows all year round. It is located 8km away from Baan I-Tong, where you will be staying, and it is best to visit with a trip on a Song-Taew.

is one of the most beautiful viewpoints in Pilok, where you can see both Myanmar and Thailand at the same time. In the photo above, you can see the sovereign land of Myanmar just behind this green hill.

place: from the road near Pilok Market
description: To get back from Pilok to Thong Pha Phum, Kanchanaburi, and eventually Bangkok, there will be a Song-Taew leaving Pilok  four times per day at 6:30 AM, 7 AM, 7:30 AM, and 8 AM. Again, there might be fewer trips depending on the day, but thankfully, they all leave on time, so you should be safe if you are at the market before 8 AM.

place: entrance of Thong Pha Phum Food Market
description: Once you arrive at Thong Pha Phum, you can catch a local bus bound for Kanchanaburi from the  right at the corner near the 7-11 store. They come every 30 minutes and it will cost you around 60-100 THB and take 2 hours to get to Kanchanaburi.

From Kanchanaburi, you should have no trouble finding a bus going to Bangkok, although I would recommend that you take a train back instead. To take the train, you will have to tell the bus driver from Thong Pha Phum to drop you off at , where you can then walk to the Nam Tok Train Station to catch the 12:55 PM train back to Bangkok.

The train will travel on the Death Railway, so you will get to see some stunning scenery along the way as well. The journey will take about 5 - 6 hours, and the train will stop at  in Bangkok. The ticket should cost around 100 THB for foreigners and 39 THB for Thais.

From Bangkok, you will then have to get to Kanchanaburi City, and then we can continue to other destinations within the province. The easiest way to get from Bangkok to Kanchanaburi without driving yourself is to take a minivan from the  across the road from the Morchit Bus Terminal.

You can reach Morchit Terminal from almost anywhere in Bangkok via either  station or BTS Morchit station, and then from there, you can take a taxi to Morchit Bus Terminal. The taxi should cost no more than 50 THB.

Right next to Roi Et Tower, you’ll find , a lake with what is arguably one of the best-developed lakeside parks in Thailand. It features fountain shows, stunning walkways over the water, and a lively evening atmosphere that reminded me of the Marina Bay area in Singapore. Yes, it's that incredible!

If you want to learn more about Roi Et and its rich history, the  is the place to be. If you look at the city on Google Maps, you’ll notice that the city center is surrounded by moats, an ancient defensive feature found in many historic cities in Thailand, highlighting just how long and storied Roi Et’s past is.

place: this street filled with street art
description: Just before you leave Roi Et City to explore other attractions around the province, be sure to stop by  created by local artists, which stretches almost the entire length of the road.

Did you know you don’t have to travel all the way to Indonesia to see Borobudur, one of the world’s most ancient and sacred Buddhist sites? You can simply visit , a forest temple about a 30-minute drive from Roi Et City Center, where you’ll find a replica of Borobudur.

Last but not least, if you’re looking for a quiet place to stroll near Roi Et City, be sure to check out . This beautiful golden pagoda is surrounded by a peaceful park, complete with a lovely pond and lush green forest. It’s the perfect spot to escape the bustling city and find solitude amidst the temple and nature.

If you’re looking for a place to have an early breakfast before exploring Roi Et, you might want to check out , a local breakfast spot that has been serving traditional dishes for generations. They offer favorites like congee, Khai Grata (fried egg on a pan), omelettes, and other Thai-style breakfast items, all at affordable prices.

If you want to try some of the famous Isaan dishes like Som Tam, Larb, and grilled pork neck with sticky rice with a local twist in Roi Et City, you should check out . This popular local restaurant originated in Roi Et and has become so successful that it now has branches in over eight provinces across Thailand.
By Flight: The fastest way to get to Roi Et is to fly from Bangkok to  operated by AirAsia and it should take about one hour. You can browse more flights via Skyscanner.

Once you arrive, you can rent a car from the airport and drive to Roi Et City, which should take about 30 minutes. There are a few car rental places at the exit terminal of the airport like , so you can pick one from there, or you can rent a car through DiscoverCars.

With such a vast area, there are also a ton of tourist spots that you can visit inside, like the famous  or the Guilin of Thailand, the water cave of Nam Ta Lu where you have to wade through gushing water as you explore the cave, and my favorite place of all, the Kri Sorn Viewpoint, one of the best hikes to do in Khao Sok.

There are many islands you can visit in Thailand but some of my favorite and most iconic islands that you have to see are located in this one region called  where you can hire a long-tail boat and explore some of the most iconic landmarks in Southern Thailand.

During your trip around Phang Nga Bay, you will also be visiting , an island, unlike anything you've seen before in Thailand.  is an old Muslim community of around 1,685 people descended from 2 seafaring families that came all the way from Java way back in the 18th Century.

Also in Surat Thani province, you will find , a beautiful and protected archipelago located off the coast of Koh Samui, home to some of the best viewpoints and islands to explore in Southern Thailand.

Islands you will be visiting are  where you will be able to explore the rich underwater world of the national park up close and personal Koh Sam Sao, where you can kayak, and Koh Mae Koh where you can hike to the emerald lake.

But nothing beats , the true highlight of the trip where you will be able to hike to the highest point of the island at Pha Chan Charat Viewpoint and get an incredible view of the whole Ang Thong Marine Park from above.
You have 2 options on what to do in Krabi. You can either choose a day of relaxation and spend your time at  or Railay Beach, have a foot massage and take it slow.

Or you can go on a full-blown adrenaline-pumping trip and join some of the cool activities you can do here like going rock climbing above one of the many limestone cliffs, kayaking around mangroves and canyons, or you can hike up to the  for a stunning view over Krabi.
The island is a small Muslim-majority island of roughly 20,000 with a rich history of seafaring commerce where you can still find traditional wooden houses on stilts along the east coast at .

The island is also home to some of the most remote beaches you can visit in the country, with many scattered across the west coast. My favorite one of all is . The sand is the finest out of all the beaches in Ko Lanta, and due to its remote location right by the southern tip of the island, the place has a tranquility vibe you can't get anywhere else.

Known for its crystal-clear turquoise waters, white sandy beaches, and vibrant marine life, Koh Lipe is often called the "Maldives of Thailand". Some of the best beaches you can visit here are  and Sunset Beach, offering not just breathtaking views and sunset/sunrise spots but also a place for swimming, sunbathing, and relaxing you can visit throughout the day

To get to Koh Lip, you have to travel to Satun first and catch a shuttle service to  where you can then catch the speedboat to Koh Lipe. It is one of the best places to visit in Southern Thailand, and if you want to explore some of the richest marine ecosystems in the country, you can travel all the way to Koh Lipe.

If you love diving,  are one of the best places to do it in Thailand. Although a bit pricier than Koh Tao in Chumphon, the marine life here is way richer, with many of the areas under the protection of the national park, preserving much of its pristine ecosystem.

Thanks to its closure during COVID and the ongoing preservation of the rich and fragile ecosystem of Phi Phi Island,  has once again become the crown jewel of Southern Thailand, boasting rich marine life, beautiful white sand beach, and some of the clearest water out there.
One of my favorite temples in Bangkok is , home to one of the largest Buddha images in Bangkok that you can see, glistening in the sun from miles away.

If you are a photographer and are looking for a nice lookout point, I highly recommend you check out the view from . You can park your car at Wat Intharam nearby and walk towards the water through the morning market.

There is also a really nice café you can check out while you are here called  and they sell local breakfast and Thai iced coffee at a really cheap price. The coffee here is only 20 THB, and you can sit by the river and enjoy the view while you have breakfast.

The temple is open from 6 AM to 9 PM every day, and there is no entrance fee. You can take the MRT to , then walk or hail a taxi to reach the temple quickly. You can also drive here and park at the temple's dedicated parking lot, which is free for the first hour and costs 20 THB for the next 2 hours.

place: the Temple of the Emerald Buddha
description: Of course, you can't make a list of the best temples in Bangkok without mentioning the crown jewel of the city,  located inside the Grand Palace, home to some of the most stunning Thai architecture in Thailand.

place: Wat Arun or the Temple of Dawn
description: Across the Chao Phraya River, you will find , an old temple that is home to the stunning white pagoda, which is prominently featured on the 10 Thai baht coin.

If you are looking for a riverside restaurant to enjoy the view of Wat Arun at sunset instead of climbing it, I recommend you check out , located right across the river from Wat Arun. They offer an unobstructed view of Wat Arun and the river. Although the food is expensive, we are paying for the exclusive view of the temple during the golden hours, which I think is well worth the price.

Another beautiful, albeit a little smaller, temple you should visit when you are in Bangkok is . Not many know about this temple, but  is home to some of the most stunning architectural designs, and best of all, it is free to enter.

was built in the 19th Century with a mixture of Thai and Western architectural styles. The exterior is clearly Thai, but once you get inside, you will notice the interior design and decor reminiscent of those found in chapels and churches in Europe, which is quite unique.

After spending the morning in the city center, it's time to head back to the Old Town once again and spend the afternoon exploring the tourist attractions that are further away from the Old Town. One of these tourist attractions is . Not many know about this temple, but  is home to some of the most stunning architecture around, and best of all, it is free to enter.

One of the best viewpoints you can get in Old Town Bangkok is from the observation deck at , or as it is known, the Golden Mountain. It is one of the best sunset spots in the area as it offers a stunning 360° panoramic view of the city.

itself was built in the 18th century during the Ayutthaya era around the ground level of the modern-day Golden Mountain, and it was not until the mid-19th century that the 80m-high hill was artificially constructed to support the golden pagoda atop. Hence, the name "the Golden Mountain."

One of the highest points in the Old City area of Bangkok is at , or as it is known, the Golden Mountain. It is one of the best sunset spots in the area as it offers a stunning 360° panoramic view over the city.
Another lesser-known temple that is located not too far from the Golden Temple is Wat Ratchanatdaram Worawihan, home to , or the Metal Castle, one of the most unique structures in Bangkok's Old City.

is a stunning structure made out of metal, consisting of 37 black spires symbolizing the 37 virtues of enlightenment. It stands on a 3-level platform with 24 spires on the first level, 12 spires on the second level, and 1 large spire at the center that reaches a height of around 36 meters.

is a stunning structure made out of metal. It consists of 37 black spires symbolizing the 37 virtues of enlightenment on a 3-level platform. The first level has 24 spires, the second level has 12 spires, and there is 1 large spire at the center that goes up to around 36 meters in height.

Located by the Dusit Palace, outside of the Old City area, you will find another stunning temple called , which is one of the few temples that were built entirely out of marble. It is also the temple that is featured on the 5 Thai Baht coin.

is built from Carrara marble imported from Italy at the end of the 19th century. The architectural style found here is a great example of Bangkok's ornate style, with high gables and multi-tiered roof design, supported by stunning marble pillars.

Buddhist temples come in many shapes and forms, and thanks to the diversity of Bangkok's population, you will also find many Chinese temples in the mix. The most beautiful and sacred Chinese temple here is , located in Bangkok's Chinatown.
You can easily reach the temple with the MRT. Make sure you get off at . You can also drive here, but you will have to park your car at Wat Khanikaphon and the parking fee is 30 THB per hour.
Bangkok is also home to a sizable Hindu community, and you will find quite a few Hindu temples here. One of the most impressive Hindu temples in Bangkok is the , located in the Silom district.
You can also drive here, but you are going to have to  and grab something from there to avoid paying parking fees.

You can't make a list of the best things to do in Bangkok without mentioning the crown jewel of the city, the , home to some of the most stunning Thai architecture in the country and a great spot to start your journey in Bangkok.

On your first day, I recommend you spend your time in the Old Town area where all the pretty architecture and temples are located, and one of the most stunning architectures of all is the crown jewel of the city, the , home to some of the most stunning Thai architecture in the country and a great spot to start your journey in Bangkok.

And there is no better way to learn about these cities and the rich history of ancient Siam than at the . The National Museum consists of several exhibitions that highlight each prominent period of Thailand.

Within , you will find a huge collection of curious artifacts and Buddhist arts dating back to the founding years of Siam from the Dvaravati, Srivijaya, Sukhothai, and Ayutthaya periods. Not only from Siam, but the artifacts and Buddhist art displayed here also come from all over Asia, from the Indian Gandhara to the Indonesian Java.

If you are looking for a place to eat lunch within the Old City area of Bangkok, you can't go wrong with the , a strip of road located not too far from Bangkok National Museum where you will find a ton of delicious local restaurants all lined up on one street for you to explore.

I highly recommend you drop by  and try their delicious Beef Pa Naeng stew with yellow rice and their mataba, which is what they are known for. You will also find plenty of other local restaurants along Phra Atthit Road, so be sure to keep an eye out when you are here.

at Phra Atthit Road is a popular restaurant among the locals. The place is known for its crispy rotis and aromatic beef stews that are to die for. It is a great place to stop for lunch while you are exploring Bangkok's Old City.

For dinner, you can never go wrong with , a foodie haven of Bangkok. Every evening, all sorts of street food stalls and carts will flock to the roadside of Yaowarat Road and start selling street food and snacks for you to indulge yourself in.

place: street food stalls right by the next street
description: Plus, you will also find plenty of cheap  where the locals usually hang out. So, even if you do not enjoy the trashy nightlife of Khaosan Road, you can at least enjoy the delicious street food there.
There are also plenty of rooftop bars across Bangkok that you can choose from, such as  in Sukhumvit or Wanderlust in Thonglor, so be sure to look around and check them out.
If you are in Bangkok during the weekend, you can shop until you drop at , one of the largest flea markets in the city where you will be able to find all sorts of things to buy for cheap.

On your second day in Bangkok, if it is Saturday or Sunday, I highly recommend you go and visit the , one of the largest flea markets in the city where you will be able to find all sorts of things to buy for cheap.

For a more high-end shopping experience, the  is one of the most prestigious shopping malls in Bangkok. It is located right at the center of the shopping mall cluster in the commercial center of Siam District in Bangkok.
If you want to taste the only Michelin Star-rewarded street food restaurant in Bangkok and money is no object,  is a place you should try once in your lifetime.

place: Jiew Bang Lumpoo Street Food Stall
description: For a more affordable street food to try in Bangkok, , located on Thani Road right next to Khaosan Road, is highly recommended.

One of the most renowned temples in Lamphun that you should visit is , situated in the center of Lamphun city. The temple, built in the early 12th century by a Hariphunchai King, was constructed to enshrine another Buddha relic discovered at this site.

There are also several temples in Lamphun that are worth a visit, such as the colorful , Wat Chammathewi, and its ancient 21-meter-high chedi, as old as the city itself. You can spend the first half of the day exploring Lamphun City and all it has to offer before we continue on to our next destination, Lampang.

After you are done in Lamphun, you can continue along the highway heading southwest of the city to , a beautiful historical city that is often considered to be the gateway province to the ancient Lanna Kingdom.

One of the first places I recommend you check out while you are here is , a stunning Buddhist temple built in the early 20th century by a Burmese immigrant. It showcases a beautiful blend of Shan and Lanna architectural styles, reminiscent of the temples in Mandalay, Myanmar.
Another cool temple to visit in Lampang is . This temple draws inspiration from the White Temple (Wat Rong Khun) in Chiang Rai.

Once you're done exploring the southern side of the Wang River, it's time to cross the river and discover the attractions on the northern side. One of the first temples you will encounter there is , a stunning Buddhist temple built in the late 19th century with teak wood, in a perfect mix of architectural styles between Lanna and Shan.

Another fascinating place to visit in Lampang is , the former residence of Louis T. Leonowens and the office of the Louis T. Leonowens company. Once a major teak logging company, it is now a museum dedicated to preserving the history of the once prosperous teak logging industry that boomed in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

If you want to learn more about the history of Lampang, you can also check out , a large traditional house built in the late 19th century by a wealthy family of Thai elites. They have preserved most of the antiques and furniture used back in the day and turned their house into a museum.

As evening approaches, you can drive over to , located 17 km southwest of Lampang. This temple, built in the 13th century in a Lanna architectural style, is one of the most revered temples in Lampang and is surrounded by a beautiful ancient wall, where you can stroll around before heading back to the city to spend the night.

If you are looking for a great place to have dinner, consider checking out . It's an old-style restaurant located inside a two-level wooden house, offering a variety of delicious dishes that you can enjoy with Thai-style rice soup. For nightlife, locals like to gather at Rgong Garden, an open-space area surrounded by pubs, restaurants, and a beer garden for you to choose from.

On your third day, before leaving Lampang, make sure to drive over to , also known as the "temple in the sky", and check it out. This incredible temple is built on top of a mountain peak, offering breathtaking views of Lampang's countryside.
As you make your way to Phrae, one of the first attractions I recommend visiting is the . This temple cave is situated on top of a sandstone mountain, tucked away in the rolling hills of Phrae.

Another impressive temple in Phrae to visit is , a newly-built temple full of stunning architecture inspired by traditional styles, with a mix of Lanna and Burmese influences, teak wood usage, and delicate sculptures.
There are also several other tourist attractions you can check out as you approach Phrae city, such as , an old temple with a unique blend of Lanna and Burmese architectural styles.

, an impressive and grand 19th-century residence, was the home of the last ruler of Phrae. It is a must-visit, especially if you want to learn more about the history of Phrae over 120 years ago, before it became part of Thailand.

Before calling it a day, be sure to check out , a newly designated national park home to beautiful red-brown rock columns created naturally through erosion, just like the Grand Canyon but way, WAY smaller. It's the Grand Canyon of Phrae.
On your 4th day, we are going to spend some time and explore Phrae before we cross over into Nan and spend a night camping under the stars at , one of the best places to stargaze in Thailand.

From the cave, it should take you about 2 hours to get to  campground where we will be spending the night. Be sure to book the campground beforehand.  is a bald mountaintop with an astounding 360° panoramic view that will allow you to see the Milky Way stretch across the sky in a way that you can't see elsewhere in Thailand.
First thing in the morning, I highly recommend you take the  and drive around the countryside of Phrae, before taking the 1342 highway back down and merge with the main highway to get to Nan.

This small loop takes you around the mountainous region of the province, allowing you to visit places such as , a replica mountain temple sharing the same name as the original temple found in Myanmar.
Another great place to drop by along the way is , a temple known for its bamboo bridges and a Buddha image surrounded by lush green rice paddies.

After spending some time at Wat Na Khu Ha, continue driving on the 1024 highway through the beautiful scenic road until you arrive at a 3-way intersection that merges with the 1342 highway. Turn left onto the 1342 highway and continue until you arrive at . Rest here and enjoy the view for a bit before you continue on.

As you merge with the main highway, drive north for about 30 minutes, and you will come across , a 1.6 km long cave illuminated with colorful lights that guide you through stunning stalactites and stalagmites until you reach a cavern with a Buddhist shrine in the middle. To be honest, I didn't have high expectations, but the place completely blew me away, so make sure to check it out on your way to Doi Samer Dao.
Let's begin with one of my favorite things to do in Surat Thani's Old Town of all, to go and feast my eyes, ears, and stomach upon the sight, the sound, and all the street food at .

There are a ton of opportunities for you to try all kinds of Thai delicious street food, many of which are only available here, like this , a staple Thai food with a little twist that makes it one of my favorite dishes here.
If you are wondering where the night market you just visited got its name from, well, if you walk all the way through the night market, you will stumble upon the  at the end.

If you just got off a long-distance bus in Surat Thani's Old Town and you are looking for a place to stretch your legs and stroll around before you start exploring the city, I highly recommend you go for a stroll at .

Oh, I love . When I was a kid, we often frequented this restaurant whenever we traveled from Tha Chang (the town where we lived) up to Ban Dorn (the local name of Surat Thani's Old Town) and we feast upon all the delicious selection of Thai food served here.

place: Plearn Jai Frozen Egg Ice Cream
description: If you are craving something sweet and refreshing dessert after a meal at Lucky Restaurant, I highly recommend you walk a little bit down the street and buy ice cream from .

In the evening, Surat Thani's Old Town is rather beautiful with many places in town lighting up with glittering lights, especially along  where you will be able to see not just have an incredible view over Tapi River but also see the beautiful lights of Sri Tapee Bridge.

If you are looking for a local dessert to try at night, I highly recommend you check out , a popular dessert street side stall that serves delicious Thai desserts like sticky rice and black beans with coconut milk, Thai grass jelly, and Red Ruby dessert, all of which are to die for. You will often see many locals waiting in line here so be sure to drop by and try them out. They are open from 6 PM to 12 AM every day except on Sundays.

While you explore Surat Thani's Old Town, be sure to drop by and check out , a beautiful and unique piece of architecture that combines the original architecture style of Srivijaya that dominated this part of modern-day Thailand way before Thailand as we know it even existed.

Last but not least, if you have more time to spare and you wish to explore the , I highly recommend you hire a long boat operator to take you along the river and check out fireflies at night that can often be found perched on the trees in the area.

You will also be able to see the lighted-up Sri Tapee Bridge at night from the boat, which is even prettier than seen from the riverfront. For a one-hour trip, it will cost you no more than 300 THB. They often operate from 6:30 PM to 9 PM every day and you can find the boat operators hanging out by the .

I traveled this route many times throughout the years, and back then, there weren't any affordable flights connecting Bangkok to Vientiane. That said, I'm happy to report that that has since changed in recent years with the opening of the Bangkok-Vientiane Route by AirAsia. The flights leave from Bangkok's  twice a day, one in the morning and one around noon.

The flight should cost about 100 - 150 USD, and you will arrive at  in about 1 hour. You can book the ticket via AirAsia website or 12Go. Once you arrive at Vientiane Airport, you can catch the airport shuttle bus, which will cost you 40,000 LAK and runs between the airport and the Central Bus Station, stopping along the way. You can see the airport shuttle bus route here.

The flight should cost about 100 - 150 USD, and you will arrive at Vientiane Airport (VTE) in about 1 hour. You can book the ticket via AirAsia website or 12Go. Once you arrive at Vientiane Airport, you can catch the airport shuttle bus, which will cost you 40,000 LAK and runs between the airport and the , stopping along the way. You can see the airport shuttle bus route here.

If you’re traveling in the opposite direction, the process is almost the same, but in the opposite direction. In the morning, you can walk to  to catch the bus to the Lao-Thailand Border Crossing. The bus should cost around 12,000 LAK (0.6 USD), and it will take about 30 minutes before you arrive at the border.

Once that is done, I caught a local bus waiting at the bus stop as I exited the immigration building and off we went to Vientiane. The bus ticket costs around 12,000 LAK (0.6 USD) and it took us about 30 minutes before we arrived at the  near Talat Sao Market. After that, I proceeded to my accommodation on foot, completing this border crossing.

Coming back was also very straightforward. I just walked to  to catch the bus to the border, costing me 14,000 LAK. I arrived at the border in 30 minutes. I then proceeded through the Lao border on foot and paid the small exit fee (100 THB for me). Then, I took the 35 THB shuttle bus across the bridge into Thailand.

Once you go through Lao immigration, you will have to pay another fee of 20 THB if you are planning to stay overnight. Once that is done, you can get on the same bus and it will take you to Vientiane in about 30 minutes, dropping you off at , near Talat Sao Morning Market, from where you can walk to your accommodation. Easy-peasy!

Once you are on the Laotian side, you can go through immigration on foot, fill in the entry form, pay the 20 THB fee, and get on bus #14 to Vientiane. The bus should cost around 12,000 LAK (0.6 USD), and it will take about 30 minutes to arrive at the , near Talat Sao Morning Market, from where you can walk to your accommodation.

You can also take a train from Bangkok to Nong Khai and switch to the international train to Vientiane to cross the border. It may be cheaper than taking a flight, but keep in mind that it will take significantly longer, and the train station is quite far away at , about 15 km from Vientiane, so you will have to get another taxi to the city.

place: this bus company office across from the 7-Eleven
description: Proceed through the Lao border on foot and pay the small exit fee (usually between 0–200 THB). Then, take the 35 THB shuttle bus across the bridge into Thailand. Once you arrive, head over to , where you can purchase a ticket (~200 THB) and catch the next bus either to Udon Thani Airport or Udon Thani Bus Station for your onward journey to Bangkok.

place: this bus company office across from the 7-Eleven
description: Once I arrived on the Thai side, I went to , where I purchased a shuttle ticket (~200 THB) to Udon Thani Airport for my onward flight to Bangkok, and that was it. Simple and straightforward.
After spending the day exploring Chiang Mai, it is time to go off into the mountains and visit the highest mountain in Thailand, , which is 2,565 meters (8,415 ft) above sea level.

place: Grand Pagodas of Doi Inthanon for a beautiful sunset and camp at
description: After the little hike, you can drive down the summit and visit the  Dong Son Campground, the campground provided by the national park for the night.
The next morning, wake up at 5 AM and travel up to the  and watch the sunrise at the Kew Mae Pan's parking lot before starting the trail.

is a 2-hour long beautiful "balcony" hiking trail along the Doi Inthanon's mountain ridge overlooking the Mae Pan River and the mountains surrounding it. It is one of the best places to watch the morning sea of fog in Thailand.

I am often skeptical about recommending places like elephant sanctuaries, as they are often not as advertised and the animals are mistreated behind the scenes. However, after days of research, I have found the place: , deep in the mountains in Mae Tang, gets my vote as one of the most ethical elephant sanctuaries out there.
For one,  is located on a huge swathe of land with a river running through, creating an almost unguarded habitat for these intelligent animals.

Before ending your second day in Bangkok, it is time to go up, and I mean really up, to one of the many rooftop bars in Bangkok and check out the city skyline at night. The place I recommend and often frequent myself is , a rooftop bar located by the Chit Lom BTS Station, one station away from Siam where we were in the morning.

While you are in the Ayutthaya Historical Park, the tour should take you to see all the important sites and tourist attractions all in one day, as well as a visit to the Ayutthaya Floating Market and other places around the ancient capital. Some of my favorite temples in Ayutthaya are , the vast temple complex of Wat Maha That, and the white pagodas of Wat Phra Si Sanphet, so be sure to drop by there when you are in Ayutthaya.

Now, if ancient cities and old structures do not seem that interesting to you, you can also opt for a day trip to the famous  to see and get on the long tail boat as it travels along a maze of canals instead.

Although it might not be as big as  and Amphawa Floating Market, it is still a nice place to experience. Since it should be midday, you can also stop here for some lunch before heading back to Bangkok.

Personally, I think the floating market is more or less a tourist trap nowadays, with everything becoming way WAY too overpriced for my liking. What I would recommend you to do instead is to visit the  and witness the market that folds into itself to let the train pass.

Our journey begins at Khlong Toei MRT station, the closest metro station to Bang Krachao. You should be able to take the MRT metro from anywhere in the city to here. From there, you can either walk about 30 minutes or hail a taxi/Grab and tell them to drop you off at .
To get to Bang Krachao, the easiest way is to take the MRT metro and get off at Khlong Toei MRT station. From there, you can either catch a taxi or walk for about 30 minutes to reach .

Your entire trip to Bang Krachao should cost no more than 100 THB (2.80 USD), excluding the MRT cost to get to . The ferry ticket is 40 THB for a round trip, while the bike rental should be around 40 THB on average. You can use the rest of the money to buy some water and snacks, and you're good to go.
You can walk to the entrance of Sri Nakhon Khuean Khan Park and rent one from  for only 40 THB per day.

Most of the bike paths are under shade, so biking around the park is a breeze even at midday. There are several stops you can drop by, like  that goes above the water, several pavilions where you can relax, and more.
One of my favorite places in Sri Nakhon Khuean Khan Park is the  located in the middle of a densely forested area. You can climb up and enjoy the view of the park from above.

If you are in Bang Krachao on the weekend, be sure to drop by  and get some lunch before ending the trip. Unlike many commercialized floating markets around Bangkok, this one is authentic, with a canal full of covered stalls selling all kinds of products, from street food to handicrafts.

Renting a car and driving there is also possible, but keep in mind that the traffic is horrible most of the time in Bangkok, and it might take you longer to drive here than to just hop on an MRT and catch the boat across. You can park your car  in front of Sri Nakhon Khuean Khan Park.

If you are driving here, after parking your car in front of Sri Nakhon Khuean Khan Park, you will find a  on the right side of the entrance gate. They may ask for an ID (you can use a local ID instead of a passport) for reassurance, and they will return it after you return the bike.

Among all the beaches I visited in Koh Chang,  is, hands down, one of the best beaches to stay at. The beach is incredibly photogenic, with its stunning view of the mountains to the left, a beautiful coastline to the right, and distant islands hovering over the horizon in the background.

You will also get to participate with the performers and take photos with the torches at the end of the show. The show often lasts around 20 minutes. When I was checking into my hotel, the staff told me that there would be a free fire dance show at  at 6:40 PM, so if you want to witness one, be sure to ask your hotel reception if your hotel is organizing one or if there is a good place to be.
Another awesome beach you should definitely visit is , a beautiful white sand beach located behind a cliff where you can rent a kayak and explore the beautiful coastline of Ko Chang at your leisure.

Along the west coast of Koh Chang, you will find plenty of beautiful beaches like Klong Prao Beach. Another awesome beach you should definitely visit is , a beautiful white sand beach located behind a cliff where you can rent a kayak and explore the beautiful coastline of Ko Chang at your leisure.
Right above Kai Bae Beach atop the cliff, you will find , a stunning observation deck offering an incredible vantage point over the coastline.
You should also check out , located right above Kai Bae Beach atop the cliff. There, you will find a stunning observation deck and a cafe with an incredible vantage point over the coastline.
Among the most impressive waterfalls on Koh Chang is , a beautiful cascade with a sizable swimming hole nestled in the heart of a jungle. It is one of the best places to visit on Koh Chang.

Koh Chang also has some really awesome waterfalls as well. The two most beautiful waterfalls on the island are , a beautiful cascade with a sizable swimming hole nestled in the heart of a jungle, and Than Mayom Waterfall, a waterfall where many Kings of Thailand have visited and imprinted their initials on.
One of my favorite places to visit in Koh Chang is the , where you will find an abandoned ship right by a remote beach on the southern tip of the island.

There is also , where you will find an abandoned ship right by a remote beach on the southern tip of the island. A fun fact: this "ship" is, in fact, not a ship but an abandoned resort located by a beautiful empty beach that you can pay to access.
Coming from the ferry pier, the first beach you will encounter is , a beautiful beach that is exactly true to its name with its incredibly fine white sand that feels amazing to walk on.
As you drive from the pier to the west coast of Koh Chang, be sure to also stop by , located right along the mountainous road approaching the White Sand Beach.

Most of the tourist attractions we've discussed so far are located on the west coast of the island, but what about the east coast? Well, I also drove around and explored this area, and one of the best places you should definitely not miss is the .
To break up your trip along the east coast, you can also stop by , a quiet fishing community with houses on wooden stilts that you can explore.
Another attraction you shouldn't miss as you explore Koh Chang's east coast is . It is one of the most beautiful and significant waterfalls on the island.

An awesome local restaurant I highly recommend is . They serve a variety of seafood dishes at a very affordable price. I ordered a Tom Yum seafood noodle that comes packed with squid and shrimp, all for only 60 THB.

To get to Ko Chang, you can take a bus or rent a car and drive straight from Bangkok to  in Trat, where you can take the ferry across to Koh Chang. The drive should take about 4 hours, and the ferry should take about 20 minutes.

Koh Chang is the largest island of the three, and it is the closest to the mainland of Trat, which makes it the easiest island to visit, taking only about 20 minutes on a 100-THB ferry from . Koh Chang is also the only island where you can take your car/motorbike across, making it the most convenient of all.

You can rent a car straight from Bangkok and explore the island with the car, or you can rent a motorbike once you are on the island. You can rent one straight from , right outside the entrance, or you can rent from your hotel. It should cost around 300 THB per day.

place: slope between the pier and the west coast
description: If you are renting a motorbike, be very careful when you get up and down the . It is one of the trickiest slope bends on the island, and sliding down uncontrollably is a risk if you are not careful!

One of the most beautiful cliffs in Chaiyaphum, and there are quite a handful here, is , an epic cliffside viewpoint with a peculiar name that would make anyone who knows Thai smile 😀 and it all comes down to the name and what it means.

There are also many other attractions in the national park you can visit like  and several other viewpoints you can check out, so be sure to spend some time and explore Sai Thong National Park. The national park is open from 6 AM to 6 PM daily, and the entry fee is 200 THB per person.

Another epic cliffside viewpoint I recommend checking out at sunset is , which offers a grand view of the mountains and forests spanning three provinces. It is located within Pa Hin Ngam National Park, and it is one of the best national parks to visit in the province.

Once you reach the end, there will be a bus stop where you can wait to catch the next bus to the other two tourist attractions in the park. After the flower fields, the tour bus will stop at , a stunning collection of large rock formations, and Pa Hin Ngam, a forest filled with oddly shaped rocks, which is where the park got its name.

If you are looking to escape the bustle and hustle of the city and be surrounded by nature, with a pristine lake filled with lotus blooms and many species of birds chirping away, then you will want to check out , located about 30 minutes' drive south of Chaiyaphum City.

There is also a  nearby where the locals like to stop for a little break while they enjoy the view over the lake, so you can check that out too. It's a lovely little peaceful place that I really like, so be sure to come check out Thung Bua Daeng Bridge while you are here. The place is open 24/7 and the entry is free with a 10 THB donation if you want to go on the bridge.

After you are done with Pha Hua Nak Viewpoint, you can drive down and drop by , a beautiful collection of rock formations that are lined up in a row for you to see. The moment I saw them, I just couldn't help but wonder how they got here in the first place! These rocks are massive and they are standing vertically instead of the usual horizontally.

While exploring Phu Laen Kha Mountain, be sure to visit , one of the province's most revered temples, located among the rolling hills and lush green forests of Phu Laen Kha Mountain, about 44 km north of the city center.

Before you head down from Phu Laen Kha Mountain, be sure to drop by , a stunning cascade with a 6-meter drop that can expand up to 50 meters in width during the rainy season (May to October), creating a beautiful wide flow over the rock plateau.

To get the most out of your Phu Lan Kha National Park ticket, be sure to visit the view from . Located on the other side of the mountain ridge, this viewpoint offers a great view of the surrounding landscape.

There is not a lot to do in Chaiyaphum City itself, as most of the province's charms lie in its incredible nature. However, if you happen to be staying overnight in Chaiyaphum and have some time to spare, you might want to drop by .

Once you arrive, you can take a motor-taxi or Sam Lor to your accommodation in the city center. From there, you can arrange a car rental and have the car dropped off at your hotel. Here are some car rental options in Chaiyaphum:  and motorcycle rental (300 THB per day). Be sure to contact them beforehand to check availability.

By Motorbike: If you are coming by bus, you can get yourself to your accommodation via motor-taxi or Sam Lor and contact  to organize the pick-up and drop-off location. It should cost about 300 THB per day.

By Car: The best way to get around Chaiyaphum is to rent a car from Khon Kaen Airport and drive to and around the province or you can rent one from  once you get to Chaiyaphum City via buses. It should cost around 1000 THB per day. You can also rent one directly from Bangkok’s airports and make the drive up. It’s a long journey, but if you have the time, there’s plenty to see along the way. You can book a rental through DiscoverCars.

Located not far from Lom Sak Old Town, you’ll find the —a large memorial and exhibition site built to honor Pho Khun Pha Mueang, the legendary founder of Mueang Rat, believed to be present-day Lom Sak. You can also learn more about his life and legacy at the Lom Sak Museum I mentioned earlier.

If you’re looking for a local and affordable place to eat in Lom Sak Old Town, be sure to check out , a beloved old noodle shop located right in the heart of the city. It’s been around for decades, and locals of all ages still flock here for their delicious egg noodle tom yum.

To finish off your lunch, you are going to want to have some local dessert to freshen you up and get you ready to continue your exploration, and there is no better place to try Thai dessert here than at , an old roadside shop that's popular among local students.

If you’re looking for a unique place to stay near Lom Sak, this is the perfect spot. You can book a room here. Even if you’re not staying overnight, be sure to check out  right next door. It’s a charming space that serves delicious local food and coffee.

By Car: For further away places, I highly recommend renting a car and driving yourself. You can  or if you prefer more reputable companies, it's best to rent one directly from Bangkok Airports and make the journey from there. You can book a rental through DiscoverCars.

The main border crossing between Thailand and Central Laos is at the  in Mukdahan, located in the Isaan region (Northeastern Thailand). The border is about a 15-minute drive from Mukdahan City, and to get there, you’ll first need to make your way to the Mukdahan Bus Terminal.

The main border crossing between Thailand and Central Laos is at the second Thai-Lao friendship bridge in Mukdahan, located in the Isaan region (Northeastern Thailand). The border is about a 15-minute drive from Mukdahan City, and to get there, you’ll first need to make your way to the .

If you're driving from Bangkok and want to leave your car in Thailand before crossing the border to Savannakhet, Laos, you can drive to  and park your car there before catching the international bus to Savannakhet.

There are  around the terminal, and you can park there for free for several nights. Just be sure not to leave any valuables in the car. I’ve done this myself, and everything was perfectly fine. And DO NOT park under a tree, unless you want your car to be covered in bird poop as mine did 😅. Let me tell you, it was a pain to clean!

On the day of the border crossing, I woke up around 7 AM and headed to the bus terminal by 8 AM. I parked my car inside the terminal area, near where the buses are stationed. You can find the exact location . Just a tip, avoid parking under any trees unless you want your car covered in bird droppings. That’s exactly what I came back to after three days in Savannakhet 😅.

How awesome is that story? It really makes you want to attend one of these rocket festivals, doesn't it? To get inside, you will have to buy the ticket from the ticket office near the . They do not sell the ticket at the museum itself, so be sure to grab one before making your way to the museum. The museum is open from 9:30 AM to 4:30 PM every day except on Tuesday, and the entrance fee is 40 THB per person.

Wiman Phaya Tan is also included in the same ticket as Phraya Kankak Museum, so you just have to pay 40 THB once and you can access both places. Again, you have to buy the ticket from  as they do not sell the ticket at the museum. The place is open from 9:30 AM to 4:30 PM every day except on Tuesday.

One of the most charming parts of Yasothon is its lovely little , known locally as Ban Sing Tha. Yasothon  is home to a handful of well-preserved colonial-era architecture, including ancient Indochinese painted wooden houses and Sino-Portuguese style buildings, which offer a glimpse into the town's rich history.

The real lion statue is still largely intact and is currently located at , about a 20-minute drive northeast of Yasothon Old Town. The stone statue is housed in a shrine, and people still make merit and pay respects to it to this day. If you have time and want to see the real deal, be sure to drop by  as well.

Right next to the Singha Tha Museum, you will find a lovely little restaurant/cafe called , where you can take a break, have breakfast, or lunch before you continue to explore the rest of Yasothon Old Town.

If you have been to one of the many boutique hotels in Thailand, you may have noticed that they often have these awesome triangular pillows that are both functional and decorative in lounges and waiting areas. These are Thai traditional pillows and they are all manufactured here in Yasothon. You can buy some for cheap at one of the many  around this area.

If you’re looking to buy some, be sure to check out Si Than Village. There are many shops lining  all the way to the end of the village, so you can drive around and pick what you like. I have had one at home for quite some time and I love it! Makes me want to buy a folded one! 😂

Did you know that Yasothon is home to one of the largest wooden Catholic churches in Thailand?  is a beautiful wooden church located within a local school in Ban Song Yae village, about a 45-minute drive northeast of Yasothon City Center.

Last but not least, if you are planning to visit Yasothon at the beginning of the rainy season, around the second week of May, on the weekends, be sure to go and attend  which often takes place in Phraya Tan Park in Yasothon City as well as other surrounding provinces.

is arguably one of the best dinosaur museums in Thailand, even more so than the one in Khon Kaen, thanks to its impressive setting, a vast collection of dinosaur fossils, and a layout reminiscent of museums you often find in Europe or the US, with wide hallways, an easy-to-follow route, and more.

No, we’re not done with dinosaurs yet. In Khon Kaen, you can hike to see dinosaur footprints, but many people were often left disappointed because of how small the footprints are due to the fact that they were left by a smaller type of dinosaur. If you want to see real dinosaur footprints, such as those left by Carnotaurus, the meat-eating dinosaurs, then you should check out the  at Phu Faek Forest Park in Kalasin.

Kalasin may not have as many tourist attractions or epic landscapes as other provinces in Thailand, but the locals definitely know how to have fun. One of the most popular spots for Kalasin residents in the evenings and on weekends is . Although it is not a beach in the traditional sense since Kalasin is landlocked, it is a recreational area by the bank of a reservoir where people can swim and relax to unwind after the day.

There are several floating markets in Thailand you can visit, and two of the most popular ones are  and Damnoon Saduak floating markets. They are the most photogenic but also the most crowded, so be sure to set your expectations accordingly.
If you have your own vehicle and prefer to visit a less touristy floating market,  is a much better place to go. It's quiet, with fewer tourists, and you can buy everything here at a local price.

In Thailand, there is also the famous  where you will find a flea market right on the train track. You can witness how they collapse the entire market to let the train through and resume their commerce after the train passes.

Now, if you decide to rent a car from Bangkok, you will be able to explore even further away. One of the most off-the-beaten-path places you can visit near Bangkok is , an adjacent province full of authentic experiences and hidden gems to be had, and it is only an hour drive away from Bangkok.

Ratchaburi is home to some of the most well-kept tourist attractions in Thailand, and one of my favorite places in Ratchaburi is , a temple hiking trail that will take you to some of the most impressive viewpoints in the province.

There are also many other tourist attractions like , an ancient temple from the Dvaravati period, even before the Ayutthaya era, and Khao Ngu Stone Park, home to the unique-looking rock formation you can stroll around in.

When you ask people about the best place to visit in Thailand for history buffs, they will often recommend either Ayutthaya or Sukhothai, which are great options in their own right. But did you know that , a city located only an hour and a half away from Bangkok, also has stunning historical ruins that predate even the ones in Sukhothai and Ayutthaya?

Lopburi is home to some of the oldest structures in the country, with many of them believed to have been constructed between the 10th and 11th centuries, such as the stunning  and King Narai's Palace.

Lopburi is not all about ancient ruins, though. The place has recently become known worldwide as the monkey city, where you will find hundreds of monkeys roaming the city freely, wreaking havoc in their little cute ways. They often congregate around the  monument located at a roundabout in the middle of the city.
is another great province to visit from Bangkok due to its proximity and easy accessibility for those seeking to escape the bustling city and experience an authentic and unique side of Thailand.
The province is home to the much-revered  pagoda, a massive ancient pagoda considered to be the tallest in the country with the top spire reaching over 120 meters.

place: Dragon Temple of Wat Sam Phran
description: You can reach the pagoda directly from Bangkok via trains, but if you want to explore more of Nakhon Pathom and visit other awesome places like the  and Wat Lam Phaya Floating Market, I highly recommend that you rent a car and drive here instead of taking the train.

Chiang Mai is a city that is always changing, and when I went back in 2025, I discovered a new and awesome art village called , which translates to "The Houses Beside the Temple". It is exactly that: a collection of wooden houses right by Wat Ram Poeng on the foothills of Doi Suthep that have been converted into an art village where you will find all kinds of art and craft shops. You can also join activities like painting, creating your own notebook, and more.

After a whole day of exploring temples, it is time to take a break and enjoy the nature of Chiang Mai. At the foothill of Doi Suthep, within the vicinity of Chiang Mai University, you will find , a massive body of water surrounded by beautiful vegetation and forest.
Do not worry. The road is in great condition throughout, and it is wide and safe enough for you to take your scooter up to Wat Doi Suthep. But before we arrive at the top, be sure to stop by  first.

Now that you've seen all the white and gold pagodas at Wat Suan Dok and Wat Phra Singh temples, how about we visit an all-silver temple, ? , also known as the Silver Temple, is located just south of the walled old city of Chiang Mai.

is known for, you guessed it, the temple design that is covered in handcrafted silver decors. From the outside wall to every corner of the temple's ordination hall, everything is covered in silver from head to toe.
Only recently discovered,  is an ancient settlement and an active archaeological site that was buried for centuries until it was rediscovered by the locals.
There are a total of 42 monuments that have been excavated at , and this ancient city covers several square kilometers. You will need your own vehicle to explore the area.

Don't worry, we will not be visiting all 42 monuments. Some monuments are quite small and may not be worth visiting. Your journey in Wiang Kum Kam will begin at  where you will get a map and access to the museum, and it is free to enter.

One of the popular places the Thai like to visit during the blooming season (December - January) is , a small mountain village up on Doi Pui mountain just behind Doi Suthep, where you will find these beautiful pink-colored Himalayan cherry blossoms blooming all over the town.

Within , you will also be able to buy souvenirs and fresh fruits like strawberries at an inexpensive price in the market. Since the flower only blooms in late December and January,  is only worth visiting if you are there during that time.

is one of the grandest temples I have ever seen in Thailand. The temple is full of over-the-top style architectures, from the pink and blue colored naga statues (that's a first!) to the black pagoda with a golden top.
Another weekend market, but this time, it is in the morning, is the  where you can buy locally-produced organic food and handicrafts that are similar to those in hipster markets in Europe.
is one of my favorite markets in Chiang Mai. It is a great place to grab a local breakfast and to buy all kinds of local products at an inexpensive price.

If you are looking for a place to watch the sunset in the city that is free, then the rooftop of  is a great place to be. Maya's large rooftop area opens around 5 PM, and from the rooftop, you will be able to see the city from above as well as Doi Suthep and other mountains around Chiang Mai.

The local event often takes place in front of the , where they will close off the road and bring out the volunteers to perform the local dance. You will also get to see several performances by local universities' students, which often consist of traditional folk dances, music, and parades.

There are 3 museums you can visit in the city center, all of which are well worth your attention. The first one I would recommend you visit is the , which is a museum dedicated to the beautiful work of art and folklore of the ancient kingdom of Lanna. Wondering what the colorful lanterns you see all over Chiang Mai and the north are all about? You will find the answer right here.

Last but not least, there's the  where you will get to learn about the history of Chiang Mai city and the notable figures that made Chiang Mai what it is today. There are also some parts of the old wall's foundation that can be seen inside the museum.

If you are in Chiang Mai on a Sunday, you will also get to see the streets of the old city center turned into a , full of shops and street food carts for you to indulge yourself in. The walking street goes for miles, and it will take you some time to cover them all.

place: Khao Soi Lung Prakit Kaat Gorm
description: One of the best Khao Soi I have ever tasted in Chiang Mai is from  or Khao Soi Lung Prakit for short, thanks to my local friends who took me to this place when I was staying in Chiang Mai.

place: Khao Soi Lung Prakit Kaat Gorm
description: is extremely popular among locals and tourists alike, so much so that the restaurant has been featured on Netflix, something that the owner is extremely proud of.

place: The Chang Phueak Suki university branch
description: Chang Phueak Suki has another branch that is more efficient, and you do not have to wait as long. It is located near Chiang Mai University.  tastes just as good, and you do not have to wait in line for hours to eat like at the Chang Phuenk Evening Market.

One of the best cafes I often frequent when I want to just sit and relax by the Ping River is . The cafe is located in this concrete house with a large platform, featuring many seating areas outside by the Ping River.
Just before you return your motorbike and end the day, on your way back from Doi Suthep, be sure to stop by the  for a little stroll and some street food.

By Train: You can also take a train from  in Bangkok to Chiang Mai Train Station, which can take anywhere from 11 to 15 hours. It is a long ride, and you are not even saving that much as the price for a ticket is about 400 to 500 THB. There are 7 trains running between the two stations from 08:30 to 22:00 every day.

Today, the Phi Ta Khon Festival is a vibrant fusion of religion, folklore, and tradition, born from a mythic past and kept alive through generations of storytelling and communal celebration. The main event takes place in , beginning at Wat Phon Chai and extending throughout much of the town's main street.

Today, the Phi Ta Khon Festival is a vibrant fusion of religion, folklore, and tradition, born from a mythic past and kept alive through generations of storytelling and communal celebration. The main event takes place in Dan Sai, Loei, beginning at  and extending throughout much of the town's main street.
The Phi Ta Khon Festival is held in Dan Sai, a small town in Loei Province, northeastern Thailand. The event starts from  and extends throughout much of the Kaew Asa Road.

Here, you’ll start to see people in colorful ghost masks and costumes entertaining visitors. You can ask to take photos with them; it’s a lot of fun! The variety of mask and costume designs is incredible. Despite the huge crowd, people moved slowly but steadily, and before long, I found myself flowing with the crowd toward , where the main event takes place.

is where the main event takes place. The temple itself is quite small, but somehow it feels more spacious than the crowded streets outside. Once I arrived at the temple, it was much easier to navigate and enjoy the event. Right from the start, traditional Isaan Mor Lam music was blasting from mobile speakers, with people dancing and enjoying themselves all around.
The Phi Ta Khon Festival is held in , a small town in Loei Province, northeastern Thailand. The event starts from Wat Phon Chai and extends throughout much of the Kaew Asa Road.

The accommodation in  is very limited and they are likely to be fully booked the moment the festival dates are announced, so you might have a better chance of finding accommodation in the nearby town. Unfortunately, the nearest large city, Lom Sak, is also an hour's drive away, so prepare for the early morning drive or you can choose to stay in smaller towns like Ban Na Dan or Phu Rua.
The Phi Ta Khon Festival is held in Dan Sai, a small town in Loei Province, northeastern Thailand. The event starts from Wat Phon Chai and extends throughout much of the .

All that exhaustion evaporated the moment I arrived at , the main street where the Phi Ta Khon Festival is held. The street was jam-packed with people, while food stalls and souvenir vendors lined both sides of the road.

place: Phi Ta Khon Dan Sai Museum
description: Inside the temple, you’ll also find the , a small yet charming place that gives you a great overview of what the festival is all about. You can see some of the older ghost masks, costumes, and other cultural artifacts on display. The museum is air-conditioned too, so naturally, you’ll find plenty of people hanging out inside, taking a break from the heat before heading back out to party.

place: Phi Ta Khon Dan Sai Museum
description: If you want to learn more about Phi Ta Khon and see how Dan Sai has celebrated the festival over the years, be sure to visit the , located inside Wat Phon Chai—the site of the main festival events.

I didn’t have the time or the mental energy to brave the traffic here, but if you do, be sure to check out . It’s a large and highly revered local temple featuring beautiful architecture, Buddhist murals, and spacious grounds perfect for exploring. The temple is open daily from 6 AM to 6 PM.

offers a fantastic panoramic view of Dan Sai town from above. During the Phi Ta Khon festival, it was far too crowded for me to stop, and this area also caused significant traffic headaches on my way back. I only caught a glimpse of the view as I passed by, but it looked stunning. If you visit outside of festival time, definitely make a stop here to enjoy the scenery.

By Bus: There’s a direct bus from  to the Dan Sai drop-off point, and the journey takes about 8 hours. There are two departures: one at 12 PM, arriving at 7:45 PM, and another at 9 PM, arriving around 4:40 AM the next day. You can check the schedule here and book tickets through the bus company’s official website, or buy them directly at Mo Chit Bus Terminal. Just be sure to book well in advance if you're planning to attend Phi Ta Khon.

One of the highlights of Chiang Khan is visiting the famous , which comes alive every evening, especially on weekends. During the day, both sides of the street are lined with charming wooden shophouses that host boutique hotels, cafés, restaurants, handicraft shops, and souvenir stores, perfect for a nice stroll during the day.

Running parallel to the Walking Street, behind Chiang Khan’s charming wooden shophouses, you’ll find the Mekong Riverfront, a lovely boardwalk along the Mekong River that begins at . The boardwalk is a wonderful spot for a relaxing stroll while enjoying river views.

If you have never witnessed or participated in almsgiving before, you can also do it in Chiang Khan. Every morning, around 6 AM, the monks from the nearby temple will walk along , where the walking street takes place, and you can donate food to the monks as they walk past your accommodation.
is one of Chiang Khan's sacred temples and highly revered by the locals. It is located right by Chiang Khan Walking Street, making it a great place to drop by and see its beautiful architecture.

Located about 7 km east of Chiang Khan,  is a scenic viewpoint featuring a charming museum, souvenir shops, and a beautiful riverfront boardwalk. From here, you’ll get a much closer view of the rolling hills of Laos across the Mekong River than you would from Chiang Khan itself.

Located in a village not too far from Chiang Khan, you will find , a house museum dedicated to preserving the history and tradition of the Tai Dam people, an ethnic group who migrated to this part of Thailand from Laos and Vietnam over a century ago via the Mekong River.

is a cozy local spot located right at the start of Chiang Khan Walking Street. It’s a great place to try local favorites like Khao Piak (Vietnamese-style noodle soup), Khai Grata (fried eggs served in a hot pan), and more, perfect for breakfast or a quick lunch.
By Bicycle: You can also rent a bicycle from most accommodations along the Chiang Khan strip for about 50 THB per day. If your accommodation doesn't offer rentals, you can check out .
By Motorbike: For farther places like Kaeng Khut Khu and the Chiang Khan Skywalk, you'll need to rent a motorbike. You can do so from , where it costs 250 THB per day.

From the bus terminal, you can easily find a motorbike taxi or a Sam Lor (three-wheeler) to take you to your accommodation. Alternatively, you can rent a car from  located inside the terminal. Rental prices for a small car start at around 900 THB per day. It's best to contact them in advance via Line, as rental shops in smaller provinces may or may not be open when you arrive.

, or "Chantabun", is an ancient waterfront community and a former residential area inhabited by Chinese and Vietnamese immigrants. This has resulted in a tightly-knit community with diverse cultures and charming traditions unlike any other Thai town you have visited before.
While we are still in town, you might as well go and check out , a lake and a large park with a diameter of around 4 km right in the city center where you can stroll around.

One of the most beautiful Chinese temples in Chantaburi is , which is one of my favorite temples. It is located right by the main road, which makes it a great place to stop along the way to Namtok Phlio National Park, our next destination.

One of the most impressive waterfalls you will find in Chantaburi is , a massive waterfall with crystal-clear water year-round. It is the perfect place to go for a refreshing swim while you are exploring Chataburi.

There are several pools you can swim in along the river stream or at the waterfall, so be sure to pack your swimsuits here. There is a  right in front of the national park, although they have limited space, so be sure to get there early.

From the entrance, it should take you about 5 minutes to reach the waterfall on foot. Along the way, you will come across some fascinating monuments, such as the . This memorial, built in 1881, commemorates the loss of Queen Sunantha, who tragically died in a boat accident on the Chao Phraya River.
Not too far from the pyramid, you will also find , a beautiful moss-covered pagoda in the middle of the forest, built during the reign of King Rama V in the late 19th century.

is, hands down, the best beach in Chantaburi, in my opinion. The beach is located inside Khao Laem Sing Forest Park, tucked behind a cape on a remote shore of Chantaburi. It is the perfect place to visit if you are looking for a quiet beach to relax for a few hours.
The forest park is open from 8 AM to 4:30 PM every day, and while you are exploring the cape, be sure to also drop by , an old defensive fort overlooking the bay below.

One of the most historical buildings near Ao Krathing that you should check out is  or Tuek Daeng, which was a military outpost built by the French when they briefly occupied Chantaburi during colonial times.
Not too far from the Red Building, you will find , another remnant from the French colonial era, with a pretty dark and twisted history.
Another nice and quiet beach in Chantaburi is , where you will find a mix of rocky and sandy beaches, which makes for an interesting sight to see.
Be sure to walk by and check out  while you are here. It is a nice place to see the beautiful rocky coastline of Kung Wiman Beach.
One of the most popular viewpoints in Chantaburi is , a roadside stop where you can enjoy the stunning view of Chantaburi's dynamic coastline.
Located right by Ban Hua Laem, a small fishing community, you will find , a viewpoint where you can see the 200-year-old white pagoda built on a rock in the middle of the ocean.
Located on the opposite side of the bay, you will find , a rich and beautiful mangrove forest with several walking paths where you can stroll around in solitude.

Thanks to the mountainous geography of northern Chantaburi, this province is home to many mountain temples that are often tucked away from the bustle and hustle of the city. The monks who founded these temples often sought peace in these remote rolling hills, and one of the best temples you should check out is .
